1:
The chapter opens on Judah's one great reformer, and the witnesses set his accession against the ruin of the north. The reformed John Gill dates the notice to the third year of Hoshea's revolt from Assyria, when the northern king had shaken off the yoke he would no longer bear. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch paint the wider canvas: at the very time the kingdom of the ten tribes was perishing, Judah lay in dependence upon Assyria, into which the ungodly policy of Ahaz had brought it; yet three years before Shalmaneser's expedition against Samaria the pious Hezekiah had ascended the throne of his father David and set on foot, with strength and zeal, the healing of Judah's wounds by exterminating idolatry and restoring the lawful worship of Jehovah. Because he was devoted to the Lord with an undivided heart, they add, the Lord acknowledged him, heard his prayer when Sennacherib overran the land, and saved Judah and Jerusalem by the miraculous destruction of the invader's host, told in this chapter and the next at 2 Kings 19.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry marvels most at the grace of it. Comparing the king's age with his father's, he reckons Hezekiah was born when Ahaz was but eleven or twelve, Providence so ordering that he might be of full age when the measure of his father's iniquity was full. His great piety was the more wonderful because his father was one of the worst of kings and he one of the best; which teaches, Henry says, that what good there is in any is not of nature but of grace, free and sovereign grace, which contrary to nature grafts into the good olive that which was wild by nature, as in Rom. 11:24. Let us not fear, he urges, a continual decay of virtue as though bad times must always grow worse, for after many bad kings God raised up one like David himself.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, surveying the whole chapter in advance, refers the reader to his earlier discussion for this third year of Hoshea, and lays out the sweep to come: the reforms, the breaking of the serpent, the revolt from Assyria, the fall of Samaria, and the Rabshakeh's insulting embassy. The other grammatical-historical voice, Charles Ellicott, lingers over the name itself. Hezekiah, in this form, means "My strength is Jah," as in Ps. 18:2, and its special fitness is exemplified by the whole of his history, in which a king who leaned on the strength of God outlived the empire that threatened him. The witnesses do not divide; together they frame the reign as a work of grace set defiantly against the tide of a perishing age.
2:
The bare regnal notice — twenty-five years old at his accession, twenty-nine years in Jerusalem, his mother Abi the daughter of Zachariah — sets the witnesses to arithmetic, for the numbers press hard against those of the previous reign. The reformed John Gill states the difficulty plainly: if Ahaz began to reign at twenty and reigned sixteen years, he died at thirty-six, so that this son must have been born to him at about eleven — which may well seem wonderful. Gill offers Grotius's easing of it, that Hezekiah had only now entered his twenty-fifth year and might be just past twenty-four, making Ahaz about twelve at the birth; and he adds that the sacred historian commonly takes away or adds the incomplete years of kings, so that Ahaz might have been nearly twenty-one at his accession and reigned almost seventeen, widening the gap to thirteen or fourteen years. Bochart and others, Gill notes, have gathered many instances of children begotten by fathers even younger.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley unties the same knot by a co-regency: it is not certain, he says, that Ahaz lived only thirty-six years, for the sixteen years assigned him may be reckoned not from the first beginning of his reign, when he reigned with his father at the twentieth year of his age, but from the beginning of his reigning alone. So the two witnesses take different roads — Gill through the rounding of incomplete years, Wesley through an overlap of reigns — to the one end, that the figures may stand without absurdity.

The grammatical-historical witnesses attend rather to the text. Keil and Delitzsch carry the record forward, noting the queen-mother's name and passing at once to the verdict that as ruler Hezekiah walked in the footsteps of his ancestor David, removing the high places and every object of idolatrous worship and adhering to Jehovah without wavering, so that the Lord made all his undertakings prosper. Charles Ellicott fastens on the mother's name: "Abi" should probably be read "Abijah," as the Chronicler has it in 2 Chronicles 29:1 and a few manuscripts here. The witnesses converge on the point beneath the numbers — that this king, whatever the exact tally of his father's years, came young to the throne and turned it at once to the service of the God of David.
3:
"He did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, according to all that David his father did." The witnesses weigh the force of that little phrase, "according to all." The reformed John Gill observes that some of the better kings of Judah are said to have done what was right, but not like David — or to have done as he did without doing according to all that he did; of Hezekiah alone is the fuller praise recorded, that he matched his father David in the whole compass of his devotion. The commendation is therefore no mere formula but a distinction, marking this king off from the merely respectable reformers who went part of the way and stopped.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fills the verdict with the history that lies behind it, drawn from the parallel record in 2 Chronicles 29. Hezekiah, he recounts, caused the priests and Levites to cleanse the holy house, which his father Ahaz had shut up and defiled — a labour that took no fewer than sixteen days. Because the temple could not be purified in time for the Passover of the first month, the king boldly kept it in the second, and sent throughout Judah and Israel to gather all who feared God, so that the feast was celebrated with great magnificence. When the people returned to their towns and villages they fell to throwing down the idol altars, images, and groves, and even to abolishing the high places, until a spirit of piety revived and a general reformation swept the land.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch gather the same acts into a single summary: as ruler Hezekiah walked in the footsteps of his ancestor David, removing the high places and the other objects of idolatrous worship, trusting in Jehovah and cleaving to him without wavering, so that the Lord prospered all his undertakings. The three witnesses do not divide. Together they read the bare formula as the seed of everything the chapter will unfold — a king whose likeness to David is proved not in words but in a cleansed temple, a kept Passover, and a kingdom purged of its idols. What the historian states in a clause, the reformation itself will spell out in deeds, and the deeds will vindicate the praise.
4:
This is the reforming heart of the chapter, and every witness gathers to it: Hezekiah removed the high places, brake the images, cut down the groves, and broke in pieces the brazen serpent that Moses had made. The reformed John Gill marks first the boldness of removing the high places, a thing the best of the earlier kings of Judah had never attempted, so that their failure is noted to their discredit. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch gather the objects of worship under their Hebrew names — the bamoth, the matseboth, and the asherah — all the apparatus of idolatry that had crept into Judah and Jerusalem under the former kings and above all under Ahaz; the one further idol singled out is the brazen serpent Moses made in the wilderness, recorded in Numbers 21:8-9, which the people in their bent toward idolatry had in time turned into an object of worship.

Over the serpent the witnesses grow eloquent. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry recalls that it was of divine institution, carefully preserved as a memorial of God's goodness in the wilderness and a standing evidence of the truth of that story in Numbers 21:9; but when the people began to burn incense to it, Hezekiah in his zeal ground it to powder and scattered it, showing them it was but Nehushtan, a mere piece of brass. If any think its honour thereby diminished, Henry answers that it was more than made up when our Saviour made it a type of himself in John 3:14; and he draws the rule, "Good things, when idolized, are better parted with than kept." He adds the warning that though Hezekiah's own father had set up the images, the son would not protect them: "We must never dishonour God in honour to our earthly parents." The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees that the serpent, once a sign of mercy, was now nothing but brass that could do neither good nor hurt, and notes that only the most frequented high places were removed, since some remained, as 2 Kings 23:13 shows.

Over the name Nehushtan the schools quietly divide. Gill, Wesley, and Henry take it as a word of contempt — simply "a piece of brass." Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott look closer at the philology: Clarke reports that no version renders it, that Jarchi read it as scornful, and that some compound it from nachash and tan as "the divining serpent"; while Ellicott judges it may really be formed from nachash, meaning "great serpent" rather than "brass-god," though the popular etymology tied it to brass. Ellicott further defends, against Baehr and Kuenen, the genuine antiquity of the relic, remarking how long Egyptian and Babylonian remains have endured. The witnesses are one on the deed and its lesson — that even a God-given thing, once worshipped, must be destroyed — dividing only over what the mocking name precisely meant.
5:
"He trusted in the LORD God of Israel; so that after him was none like him among all the kings of Judah, nor any that were before him." The witnesses read this as the inward spring of all the reforms just told. The reformed John Gill fixes on the object of that trust: Hezekiah leaned on the Lord to be his protector, and had no dependence on idols as an arm of flesh. He notes the Targum's reading, that the king trusted in the Word of the Lord — not in Nehushtan, but in Him whom the brazen serpent had typified, even the Word and Son of God, his only Saviour and Redeemer. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws the king's character out in an ordered chain: he trusted in the Lord, he clave to him, he was steady in his religion and departed not from following him, he kept God's commandments — and the consequences answered, for the Lord was with him and he prospered whithersoever he went.

The great difficulty of the verse is its superlative — "none like him" — which is said again of Josiah, and the witnesses labour to reconcile them. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, answers that there is no contradiction with 2 Kings 23:25: Hezekiah was pre-eminent for his trust in Jehovah, Josiah for his strict adherence to the Mosaic law, so that each held a distinct crown. Keil and Delitzsch agree exactly, observing that the verdict here rests on the word batach, confidence, in which Hezekiah had no equal, while of Josiah the same words are used of his conscientious keeping of the law — so that Thenius is wrong to find a clash.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds the same resolution from his own angle: the "before him" reaches only over the kings of Judah, for David and Solomon were kings of all Israel; and while the like is said of Josiah, each excelled the other in several respects — Hezekiah in this, that he fell upon the work at the very beginning of his reign and dared to remove the high places, which Josiah did only after his example. Wesley notes too that Hezekiah trusted without calling in the foreign succours that Ahaz, and before him Asa, had sought. The witnesses converge: the king's singular greatness lay not in arms or wealth but in a trust that leaned on no idol and no ally, and that very trust the chapter will presently put to the fiercest test.
6:
"For he clave to the LORD, and departed not from following him, but kept his commandments, which the LORD commanded Moses." The witnesses read this verse as the ground of the praise just given, the constancy that makes trust more than a moment's impulse. The reformed John Gill glosses the clinging simply: Hezekiah clave to the Lord's worship and service, or, as the Targum has it, to the fear of the Lord — a fidelity that was fixed and lifelong, not a passing zeal that cools.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch weigh the Hebrew verb, dabak, the same word used of cleaving in 1 Kings 11:2: he adhered faithfully to Jehovah and departed not from him — which is to say, they explain, that he never at any point gave himself up to idolatry. The negative is as important as the positive; the whole northern kingdom had just fallen for the opposite, and here stands a king of whom it can be said that he did not once turn aside. Because he so held fast, they add, carrying the thought into the next verse, the Lord gave him success in all his undertakings.

Charles Ellicott, the other grammatical-historical voice, parses the two clauses as feeling and practice joined. "For he clave" he renders "and he held fast" — Hezekiah's pious affection; "but kept" he renders "and he kept" — Hezekiah's obedient practice; and he observes that the commandments specially in the writer's mind were those against polytheism, the very idolatry the king had purged. The witnesses are careful, too, with the last clause, "which the LORD commanded Moses": the rule Hezekiah kept was no invention of his own zeal but the ancient law given at Sinai, so that his reformation was in truth a restoration, a return to the standard the nation had abandoned. The witnesses do not divide. Together they let the verse do a quiet but weighty work: it guards the praise of the previous verse from being mistaken for the flush of a young reformer, and grounds it instead in a settled, obedient perseverance. Hezekiah's greatness was not that he began well but that he did not depart; and it is on that unwavering adherence, the witnesses agree, that the favour and prosperity of God will presently be seen to rest.
7:
"And the LORD was with him; and he prospered whithersoever he went forth: and he rebelled against the king of Assyria, and served him not." The witnesses read the prosperity as the fruit of the fidelity just described, and the revolt as its boldest expression. The reformed John Gill notes briefly, with the Targum, that the Word of the Lord was for his help. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the causal chain: because Hezekiah clave to the Lord, the Lord gave him success in all he undertook, using the verb of 1 Kings 2:3; and this success extended even to his rebellion against the king of Assyria, to whom he paid no more tribute. It was through Ahaz, they observe, that Judah had been brought into this dependence, and Hezekiah freed himself from it by refusing tribute — probably after the departure of Shalmaneser from Palestine, perhaps not until after that king's death — so that Sennacherib later made war to subjugate Judah afresh, as the narrative resumes at v.13.

Charles Ellicott renders the promise vividly — "whithersoever he would go forth he would prosper" — comparing the prospering of 1 Kings 2:3 and Proverbs 17:8, and taking "going forth" of any enterprise, especially going out to war. He cautions that the mention of the revolt here does not fix it at the reign's beginning, for the first twelve verses are a preliminary sketch of the whole history, the subject being taken up at large only from v.13.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley defends the act at length. Hezekiah shook off a yoke to which his father had wickedly submitted and reassumed the full sovereignty God had settled in the house of David; and his case, Wesley insists, differs wholly from that of Zedekiah, who is blamed for rebelling against Babylon — for Zedekiah had bound himself by a solemn oath, and God had actually given the dominion to Babylon and commanded submission, whereas of Hezekiah no such oath or command is recorded. That the revolt was no sin, Wesley argues, is plain from this: God owned and assisted him in it, and never reproved him for it in Isaiah's messages, though he did reprove his later vainglory, as 2 Chronicles 32:25 records. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that the surest way to liberty was to serve God, and that a throne which leaned on him could safely defy an empire.
8:
"He smote the Philistines, even unto Gaza, and the borders thereof, from the tower of the watchmen to the fenced city." The witnesses read this campaign as the recovery of what apostate Ahaz had lost. The reformed John Gill recalls that the Philistines had in the father's time invaded Judah and seized many towns; Hezekiah now recovered them and drove the enemy back to their own territory, of which Gaza was the border. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take the phrase "from the tower of the watchmen to the fenced city" as denoting all the towns from the least to the greatest, as at 2 Kings 17:9; the king thus chastised the Philistines for their inroad in the days of Ahaz, wrested back the cities they had taken according to 2 Chronicles 28:18, and laid waste their country to Gaza, the most southerly of the Philistine cities, as at Joshua 13:3. This, they judge, probably fell after the defeat of Sennacherib, comparing 2 Chronicles 32:22-23.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke treats the same phrase, "from the tower of the watchmen," as proverbial, already met at 2 Kings 17:9: Hezekiah reduced every kind of fortification, so that nothing was able to stand before him. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, likewise renders the opening emphatically — "He it was who smote" — takes the reduction of Philistia to have followed the retreat of Sennacherib, comparing 2 Chronicles 32:22 and Isaiah 11:14, and marks Gaza as the southernmost point, the entire Philistine land being ravaged by the forces of Judah.

The witnesses do not divide. Together they let the verse round off the preliminary sketch of Hezekiah's prosperity: the king who purged the idolatry his father had brought in also recovered the territory his father had lost, and the God who was with him at home was with him in the field. The reticence of the notice — a whole war compressed into a single sentence — is itself telling, for the historian is hastening on to the graver trial, the Assyrian invasion that the next verses begin to unfold, in which the same trust that won these victories will be assailed to its very foundations.
9:
The narrative turns back to the fall of Samaria, and the witnesses ask why it is told a second time. The reformed John Gill notes only that the siege began at the opening of Hezekiah's fourth year, the seventh of Hoshea. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the reason for the repetition: the destruction of the ten tribes, already related from the northern annals in 2 Kings 17:3-6, is here set down again from the annals of Judah, in which this catastrophe stood as an event memorable to the whole covenant-nation, not to the north alone.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the lesson the placement invites. The kingdom of Assyria, he observes, had now grown formidable, though scarcely heard of before — such are the changes in the affairs of nations, the despicable becoming dreadful and the great brought low. The recital of Samaria's fall is repeated here, he suggests, for three ends: it stirred Hezekiah and his people to purge out idolatry with the more zeal, for when a neighbour's house is on fire and one's own in danger it is time to cast away the accursed thing; it was a calamity Hezekiah lamented but had no strength to prevent, for though the ten tribes had often vexed the house of David he could not rejoice in the ruin of the seed of Israel; and it laid Judah itself the more open to the invader. Of the ten tribes it is said that they would neither hear God's commandments nor do them, and Henry marks how many will give God the hearing who will give him no more, as in Ezekiel 33:31, while these, resolved not to do their duty, cared not even to hear of it.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke passes over it briefly, noting only that this history has already been given at 2 Kings 17:3. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the fall of the sister kingdom is set here deliberately as a warning held up before Judah: the very sin Hezekiah was purging had just brought a nation to captivity, and the smoke of Samaria's ruin was meant to be read by Jerusalem as both a spur to reform and a foreboding of what unremoved idolatry must finally cost.
10:
"And at the end of three years they took it: even in the sixth year of Hezekiah, that is the ninth year of Hoshea king of Israel, Samaria was taken." The witnesses attend to the chronology and to a nicety of the verb. The reformed John Gill explains "at the end of three years" to mean at the first end, the beginning of them — the siege having run into its third year — and synchronises the sixth year of Hezekiah with the ninth of Hoshea, the year the city fell. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the verse forward into the deportation and the reason for it, and note that the fuller and parallel account of the whole Assyrian crisis stands in Isaiah 36-39 and, with certain supplements, in 2 Chronicles 32.

Charles Ellicott, the other grammatical-historical voice, presses the small word "they." "They took it," he insists, means the Assyrians took it, and this reading is preferable to the singular "he took it" of the Septuagint, Syriac, and Vulgate — for it was Sargon, not Shalmaneser, who actually took the city, Shalmaneser having died or been supplanted during the siege. He faults Schrader for calling the plural "a certainly false pronunciation" of the Hebrew, and points out that 2 Kings 17:6, cited as decisive, speaks only of "the king of Assyria," not of Shalmaneser by name. So a real question hangs on the verb: whether the fall of Samaria is to be laid to the king who began the siege or to the king who ended it, and whether the text should read a plural agent or a single one.

The witnesses do not otherwise contend. They agree that the notice is set here in the annals of Judah to fix the exact year of the northern kingdom's end against the reign of its godly southern contemporary, and that the reader is meant to feel the nearness of the disaster — Samaria fallen in Hezekiah's sixth year, while Jerusalem, purged and trusting, stood on. The precision of the dating is not pedantry but testimony: the covenant-nation's historian will have the day of the ten tribes' ruin recorded to the year, that later generations may know when, and under what king, the sister kingdom passed into the hand of Assyria and out of the land.
11:
"And the king of Assyria did carry away Israel unto Assyria, and put them in Halah and in Habor by the river of Gozan, and in the cities of the Medes." The witnesses are sparse here, the deportation having been told already, yet they let the bare geography speak. The reformed John Gill notes only, of the places into which the exiles were dispersed, that they are named here and have been treated where the same removal was first recorded. The place-names themselves carry the weight — Halah, Habor by the river of Gozan, and the cities of the Medes — for they mark how far and how finally Israel was scattered, planted among strangers in the heart of the empire, far from the land of the covenant.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, whose comment runs across these verses, join this deportation to the reason that follows in the next verse: the king of Assyria carried Israel away because they obeyed not the voice of the Lord their God but transgressed his covenant. The removal is thus no accident of empire but the executed sentence of the God whose covenant they broke; the same annals of Judah that dated the fall of the city now record the dispersal of its people, that the covenant-nation might read in it the wages of apostasy.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, gathering these verses together, sees in the captivity a calamity that Hezekiah lamented but could not prevent, and a warning laid at Judah's own door. The ten tribes are led away for the very sin Hezekiah was labouring to purge from the south, so that the long procession into Assyria becomes a sermon in action. The witnesses do not divide. Together they let the verse stand as the quiet, terrible close of a kingdom: the people whom God had brought up out of Egypt are carried down into the cities of the Medes, and the land that was their inheritance keeps them no longer. What the earlier chapter announced as judgment, this verse records as accomplished fact — Israel gone, and only Judah, for a season, left to hear the warning.
12:
"Because they obeyed not the voice of the LORD their God, but transgressed his covenant, and all that Moses the servant of the LORD commanded." The witnesses read this verse as the epitaph on the northern kingdom, the reason written over its grave. The reformed John Gill glosses the disobedience simply: they obeyed not the voice of the Lord as it came in his law and by his prophets — the double witness of the written word and the living messengers, both alike despised.

Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, adds the literary observation that this remark properly belongs to the historical abstract from which the compiler drew the sketch of the first twelve verses; Thenius calls it "the theme" that suggested the fuller reflections of 2 Kings 17:7-23, and it may itself have been prompted by passages of the Law and the Prophets. He would omit the "and" before "all," with the versions, reading "all that Moses commanded" as in apposition with "his covenant," and renders the closing clause literally, "and they hearkened not, and did not" — a bare, doubled negative that leaves no room for excuse.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry seizes on that same doubling. It is said of the ten tribes, he notes, that they would neither hear God's commandments nor do them; many will content themselves with giving God the hearing who will give him no more, as in Ezekiel 33:31, but these, being resolved not to do their duty, did not care so much as to hear of it. The witnesses do not divide. Together they make the verse the moral hinge of the whole recital: Samaria did not fall to the mere might of Assyria but to the justice of God, and the cause was neither the empire's strength nor Israel's weakness but Israel's settled refusal to hear and to obey. The historian will not let the captivity be read as misfortune; he writes it down as the covenant's own sanction, executed at last upon a people who broke it with their eyes open — and he sets it where Judah, still standing, could not fail to read the lesson.
13:
"Now in the fourteenth year of king Hezekiah did Sennacherib king of Assyria come up against all the fenced cities of Judah, and took them." With this verse the chapter's great crisis begins, and the witnesses divide their attention between chronology and providence. The reformed John Gill sets the date briefly: it was eight years after the captivity of Israel. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark the opening of the main narrative — Sennacherib invades Judah and threatens Jerusalem — and remind the reader that the parallel and elaborate account runs through Isaiah 36-39 and, in brief, through 2 Chronicles 32.

Charles Ellicott, the other grammatical-historical voice, presses a chronological difficulty he refuses to smooth over. The fall of Samaria is fixed at 722-721 B.C., Hezekiah's sixth year; his fourteenth would therefore be 714-713, yet Sennacherib's own monuments date the expedition against Judah to 701. This divergence, Ellicott insists, is remarkable and must not be explained away, for the Assyrian documents are strictly contemporary, while the Books of Kings were compiled long after and have reached us through innumerable transcriptions. He identifies the invader from his own annals as Sin-ahi-erib, son and successor of Sargon, who reigned from 705 to 681 and invaded Judah in his third campaign, taking the fenced cities of which he boasts, as noted at 2 Chronicles 32:1.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry look past the dates to the divine purpose. Wesley takes "took them" to mean many of them, universal words being often so used, and reads the success God granted Sennacherib as given to lift him up to a more shameful destruction, to humble and chastise God's own people for their manifold sins, and to gain an eminent occasion for the miraculous deliverance he designed. Henry likewise weighs how much Hezekiah lost — the frontier towns and garrisons falling into the enemy's hand, the confusion that Isaiah describes at Isaiah 10:28-31 — and reads the calamity as a trial of the king's faith and a chastening of a people who had not heartily parted with their idols. The witnesses do not finally divide; the chronological crux is Ellicott's alone, while all agree that behind the Assyrian's advance stood the hand of God, arming the rod even as he purposed to break it.
14:
"And Hezekiah king of Judah sent to the king of Assyria to Lachish, saying, I have offended; return from me: that which thou puttest on me will I bear." Here the witnesses part company over the king's soul. The reformed John Gill reads the embassy as a lapse: Hezekiah had at first made provision to defend himself and encouraged his people not to fear, but when he saw the fortified cities fall he was disheartened and sued for peace, judging it wiser to buy off the invader than to expose his capital — in which, Gill says plainly, he betrayed much weakness and distrust of the power and providence of God. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry is sharper still, crying, "Where was Hezekiah's courage? where his confidence in God? Why did he not advise with Isaiah before he sent this crouching message?" The submission cost him dear and made him never the safer.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch report the same acts without the reproach. On word of Sennacherib's approach Hezekiah at once secured Jerusalem, fortifying the city and stopping the upper Gihon to deny the besiegers water, as 2 Chronicles 32:2-8 and Isaiah 22:8-11 confirm; only then, doubting he could hold out against so great a force gathered at Lachish, did he send to negotiate. The confession "I have sinned," they note, is the language of a vassal owning his revolt. Charles Ellicott adds the topography and the numbers: Lachish, Um-Lakis, guarded the road to Egypt, which was Sennacherib's true objective; "I have offended" is literally "I have sinned," a term used of revolts in the Assyrian inscriptions; and where the text names three hundred talents of silver and thirty of gold, Sennacherib's own record says eight hundred talents of silver — a difference Ellicott resolves by the lighter Babylonian talent, the sum being about a seventh less than Pul exacted from Menahem in 2 Kings 15:19. Wesley notes only that three hundred talents came to above two hundred thousand pounds. The witnesses thus divide over the heart of the deed — a failure of faith to Gill and Henry, a prudent submission to Keil and Delitzsch — while agreeing on its price and its futility, for the money bought no peace at all.
15:
"And Hezekiah gave him all the silver that was found in the house of the LORD, and in the treasures of the king's house." The witnesses read this stripping of the treasuries as the bitter reckoning of the tribute. The reformed John Gill notes that to make up the three hundred talents the king exhausted both the temple and the palace stores — a resource the kings of Judah had been driven to more than once before in times of distress, so that the sacred and the royal wealth alike drained away to appease the invader.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch look ahead to what the payment purchased, which was nothing. Though Sennacherib took the money, they observe, he did not depart as he had surely promised, but, emboldened the more by this submissiveness, sent a detachment of his army against Jerusalem and summoned Hezekiah to surrender the capital — dispatching Tartan, Rabsaris, and Rabshakeh, of whom Rabshakeh alone is named in the parallel of Isaiah as the chief speaker. So the tribute of this verse becomes, in their reading, the very thing that whetted the enemy's appetite. Charles Ellicott adds only the small gloss that "the silver" here means simply the money, the current coin of the realm gathered up to the last.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry weighs the act with a mixture of censure and sympathy. He faults the fear that emptied the public treasures, yet allows that Hezekiah, taking his father David for his pattern, might think it neither impious nor imprudent to give a part for the preservation of the whole — as David had eaten the shewbread in necessity. His father Ahaz had plundered the temple in contempt of it; Hezekiah, with all reverence, did but borrow from it in an exigency, resolving to restore it in full when he could. The witnesses do not finally divide over the fact, only over its blame; and all of them let the emptied treasuries stand as the measure of how much a frightened faith will pay, and how little such payment buys when the enemy has no intention of keeping faith in return.
16:
"At that time did Hezekiah cut off the gold from the doors of the temple of the LORD, and from the pillars which Hezekiah king of Judah had overlaid, and gave it to the king of Assyria." The witnesses dwell on the pathos of a godly king dismantling his own adornment of the house of God. The reformed John Gill explains simply that these were the plates of gold with which the doors had been covered, which the king now scraped off, as the Targum interprets the verb — the very gold that had beautified the sanctuary now peeled away to fill an Assyrian's coffers.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the wording. "Cut off the gold" is literally "trimmed" or "stripped" the doors, the same word used in 2 Kings 16:17 of Ahaz's plundering of the temple furniture; the leaves of the sanctuary doors had been overlaid with gold, as 1 Kings 6:18 and its neighbours record. Hard necessity, Ellicott says, drove Hezekiah to strip off this gold, and with it the gold with which he had himself plated the "pillars" — better, the framework or supports of the doors, though some take the word of the door-posts only. There is a quiet irony the witnesses feel: the reforming king who had cleansed and enriched the temple must now, under the same devotion turned to fear, undo his own work with his own hands.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, treating these verses together, sets the deed beside his father David's eating of the shewbread: though the temple sanctified the gold that Hezekiah had dedicated, the necessity being urgent, he thought it neither impious nor imprudent to give a part for the preservation of the whole. Where Ahaz had robbed the temple in scorn, Hezekiah did but borrow from it in an exigency, meaning to repay. The witnesses do not divide. Together they let the stripped doors testify how heavy the Assyrian yoke pressed, and how a faith not yet come to its full strength will spend even the gold of God's house to buy a peace that the next verse will show was never on offer.
17:
"And the king of Assyria sent Tartan and Rabsaris and Rabshakeh from Lachish to king Hezekiah with a great host against Jerusalem." The witnesses open with the Assyrian's treachery. The reformed John Gill marks how false and deceitful he was, for notwithstanding the great sum he had taken, he sent three generals to besiege Jerusalem while he himself pressed the siege of Lachish; Rabshakeh, the chief speaker, would deliver the taunting message recorded almost word for word in Isaiah 36. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry is roused to indignation: is this the great king, the king of Assyria? Never call him so; he is a base, false, perfidious man, who took Hezekiah's money on condition of withdrawing and then advanced against his capital and never returned the money either. Henry notes the prudence of the king, who declined a personal parley and sent three commissioners, charged not to answer, that they might not provoke a madman.

The witnesses agree that the three names are titles of office, not personal names. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, following Calmet, glosses them: Tartan, who presided over the tribute, a kind of chancellor; Rabsaris, the chief of the eunuchs; Rabshakeh, master of the king's cup or wine-cellar. Charles Ellicott confirms the point from the monuments — Tartan is the Assyrian tur-ta-nu, the commander-in-chief, seen also in Isaiah 20:1; Rabsaris and Rabshakeh are further titles, the latter really the Hebraised rab-sak, "chief officer," so that "cup-bearer" is only the surface sense; and in Isaiah 36:2 Rabshakeh alone is named, as chief speaker.

On the setting the witnesses fill in the scene. They stood, Ellicott notes, by the conduit of the upper pool on the highway of the fuller's field, the place of Isaiah 7:3, where the washermen spread their clothes to dry, as Clarke adds — the aqueduct perhaps seized to distress the city. Keil and Delitzsch stress the perfidy behind it all: Sennacherib took the money yet did not depart, but, emboldened, sent to demand the surrender of the capital. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees that, having received the tribute on terms of withdrawing, he broke his faith with Hezekiah, thereby justifying the revolt and preparing the way for his own destruction. The witnesses do not divide; together they present a treaty betrayed and an insulting embassy sent, so that the stage is set for the great trial of Hezekiah's faith, which is not force of arms but a war of words against the confidence of God's people.
18:
"And when they had called to the king, there came out to them Eliakim the son of Hilkiah, which was over the household, and Shebna the scribe, and Joah the son of Asaph the recorder." The witnesses read the exchange of persons as a matter of royal dignity and prudence. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain that Hezekiah counted it beneath his dignity to treat personally with Sennacherib's generals, and so sent out three of his leading ministers: Eliakim son of Hilkiah, captain of the household, who had lately received the office in Shebna's place, as Isaiah 22:20-21 tells; Shebna, still secretary of state, the sopher of 2 Samuel 8:17; and Joah son of Asaph, the chancellor or recorder, the mazkir of 2 Samuel 8:16. To these three, they add, Rabshakeh made the speech that follows, laboring to show that Hezekiah's confidence was vain, since neither Egypt, nor his God, nor his own forces could defend him.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads a sinister motive into the summons: the Assyrians wished the king to come out that they might seize his person. He renders the offices in the terms of his own day — Eliakim as lord chamberlain, over the household; Shebna as the king's secretary; Joah as the writer of the public annals. Charles Ellicott agrees that the envoys demanded a parley with Hezekiah himself, and that the king sent out his chief ministers instead, comparing the list of high officers in 1 Kings 4:1-4 and referring for Eliakim and Shebna to the fuller notices of Isaiah 22:15.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry rounds out the picture: Rabshakeh had the impudence to desire audience of the king himself at the conduit without the walls, but Hezekiah had the prudence to decline a personal treaty and sent his prime ministers to hear what he had to say. The witnesses do not divide. Together they show a king who will not lower his throne to bandy words with insolent envoys, and who interposes his most trusted servants between himself and the enemy's provocation — a small act of wisdom that keeps the dignity of the crown intact even as the storm of the Rabshakeh's blasphemy is about to break over the wall.
19:
"And Rabshakeh said unto them, Speak ye now to Hezekiah, Thus saith the great king, the king of Assyria, What confidence is this wherein thou trustest?" The witnesses expose both the swagger of the title and the sneer of the question. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that "the great king" was the standing style of the Assyrian, Babylonian, and Persian monarchs, as Ezekiel 26:7 and Daniel 2:37 show, because subject kings were their vassals; and the opening question means, in effect, how worthless is this confidence of yours! Charles Ellicott enlarges the grandiloquence from the inscriptions, where Esarhaddon styles himself "the great king, the mighty king, the king of multitudes," and where Hosea gives the Assyrian the title "king of princes" in Hosea 8:10; he notes that Tiglath-Pileser records sending a rab-sak as envoy, and that Rabshakeh, as Thenius guessed, may have been a better master of Hebrew than his fellows.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke feels the insult in the very grammar. The words are excessively insulting, he says, and he takes the Hebrew bittachon as a diminutive, a deliberate belittling — "what little, foolish, unavailing cause of confidence is this to which thou trustest?" — designed to express the utmost contempt for Hezekiah's God. John Wesley cuts to the theological heart: "But what are the greatest men when they come to compare with God, or when God comes to contend with them?"

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the strands into a meditation on pride. See how Rabshakeh magnifies his master, calling him again and again "that great king," making an idol of the prince whose creature he was: God is the great King, but Sennacherib was in his eye a little god, and he would fill the people with the same veneration and so frighten them into submission. Yet to those who by faith see the King of kings in his power and glory, Henry says, even the king of Assyria looks mean and little, as Psalms 82:6-7 declares of all such petty gods. The witnesses do not divide; together they hear in the Rabshakeh's first sentence the two notes that will run through the whole speech — the exaltation of the empire and the belittling of trust in the living God.
20:
"Thou sayest, (but they are but vain words,) I have counsel and strength for the war. Now on whom dost thou trust, that thou rebellest against me?" The witnesses fasten on the scornful parenthesis. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render it exactly: if Hezekiah speaks of counsel and might for battle, that is only a "word of the lips," a saying that comes from the lips and not from the heart, the seat of the understanding — a foolish and inconsiderate boast, such as Proverbs 14:23 and Job 11:2 condemn. The "now" that follows means, in effect, we shall see on whom you relied when you dared to rebel. They note in passing that the form here is the more original against the variant of the parallel in Isaiah.

Charles Ellicott renders the taunt in the same spirit: "thou hast said — a mere lip-word it was" — that is, insincere language, an utterance the king himself knew to be hollow, what we should call lip-service. He corrects the margin of the older versions and lets the sting stand: the Assyrian will not so much as grant that Judah's talk of counsel and strength is honestly meant.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry answers the sneer with the fact. Hezekiah did indeed have counsel and strength for the war, as 2 Chronicles 32:3 shows in the stopping of the waters and the strengthening of the walls; but Rabshakeh turns it all off with a slight, calling it vain words and challenging the king, with the utmost haughtiness, to produce two thousand men able to ride, and he would furnish the horses. Thus, Henry observes, he thinks to run Hezekiah down with confidence and banter, laying him any wager that one captain of the least of his master's servants could master him and all his forces. The witnesses do not divide. Together they show the enemy's method laid bare: not to meet Judah's real preparations, which were genuine and diligent, but to mock them as empty breath, and to shift the ground at once from arms to the deeper question — "on whom dost thou trust?" — which is the question the whole speech is bent on answering with despair.
21:
"Now, behold, thou trustest upon the staff of this bruised reed, even upon Egypt, on which if a man lean, it will go into his hand, and pierce it." The witnesses admire the aptness of the figure even as they expose its malice. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch unfold it: Egypt is that broken reed which runs into the hand of anyone who would lean on it thinking it whole, and pierces it through — a figure repeated in Ezekiel 29:6-7, and fitly chosen, since the Nile, the emblem of Egypt, is rich in reeds. What Rabshakeh here throws in Judah's teeth, they note, Isaiah had already pressed upon the people in Isaiah 30:3-5, to warn them against the very trust in Egypt that one party in the nation was cherishing.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds the point of history that gave the taunt its edge: Egypt had already been greatly bruised and broken through the wars the Assyrians had carried on against it, so that to lean on such a power was to lean on a thing already cracked. Charles Ellicott refines the image itself, preferring "cracked" or "flawed" to "bruised," for the idea is of a reed that splits and pierces the hand resting on it, as the language of Isaiah 42:3 suggests; and he gathers the passages in which the prophet denounces Judah's Egyptian hopes as a want of faith in Jehovah, among them Isaiah 31:1-4.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the reed to a sermon. Those who trust to any arm of flesh, he says, will find it no better than a broken reed, which not only fails a man when he leans on it but runs into his hand and wounds him; so had the king of Assyria himself proved to Ahaz, who trusted in him and was distressed rather than strengthened, as 2 Chronicles 28:20 records. But God, Henry adds, is the rock of ages. The witnesses do not divide. Together they let the enemy's jeer become, against his intention, a true word: Egypt was indeed a broken reed, and Judah's only safe stay was the God whom Rabshakeh mocked.
22:
"But if ye say unto me, We trust in the LORD our God: is not that he, whose high places and whose altars Hezekiah hath taken away?" Here the Assyrian turns his cleverest stroke against the king's very reformation, and the witnesses expose the malice in it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that Judah had in Jehovah a far stronger ground of confidence, and that Rabshakeh, from his heathen standpoint, sought to shake even this by representing the abolition of the high-place altars as an affront to the God so worshipped — as though Hezekiah, in confining worship to the one altar at Jerusalem, had robbed the deity of his due reverence. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke calls the argument artfully malicious: many of the people had sacrificed to Jehovah on the high places, and Hezekiah had removed them as incentives to idolatry, following v.4; Rabshakeh insinuates that by so doing the king had offended Jehovah and stripped the people of their religious rights, so that they could look for neither God's blessing nor their king's favour.

Charles Ellicott observes that this is precisely the construction a heathen would naturally put upon the abolition of the local sanctuaries recorded in v.4 and 2 Chronicles 31:1; the Assyrians, watching their vassal with jealous interest, had evidently heard of the reformation. He notes the shift of address from Hezekiah to the ministers and garrison, and the parallel in Isaiah 36:7, and reads the closing words as insisting that before this one altar, at Jerusalem, the people were now confined.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley marvels at the presumption: boldly he speaks of things he understood not, judging the great God by their petty gods, and measuring piety by the multitude of altars, as the heathen did. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry drives it home — one of the best deeds Hezekiah ever did is here misconstrued as impious and profane by one who did not know the law of the God of Israel; and if what is truly pleasing to God be represented by malicious men as an offence to him, we must not think it strange. The witnesses do not divide; together they show the enemy weaponizing the reform itself, turning obedience into a supposed sacrilege to unnerve the faithful.
23:
"Now therefore, I pray thee, give pledges to my lord the king of Assyria, and I will deliver thee two thousand horses, if thou be able on thy part to set riders upon them." The witnesses hear in this a jeer at Judah's military weakness. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the challenge: enter into contest with my lord, and I will give thee two thousand horses, if thou canst but find horsemen to mount them. The meaning, they explain, is not that Hezekiah could not raise two thousand men at all, but that he could not produce so many trained to fight as cavalry; and the taunt runs on — how then wilt thou repulse a single one of the smallest lieutenants of my lord, a mere pachath or under-governor?

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke feels the sting plainly: it is another insult, as if to say, were I to give thee two thousand Assyrian horses, thou couldst not find riders for them; how then canst thou think to stand against even the smallest division of my troops? Charles Ellicott surveys the disputed opening word, which some render "give pledges," others "make a compact with," or, more literally, "mingle with," "have dealings with," and which Gesenius takes as "join battle," while Cheyne prefers "lay a wager"; whatever the precise force, the rab-sak sneers at Hezekiah's want of cavalry, an arm in which the Assyrians were pre-eminently strong, and hints that even if the horses were supplied, Judah could muster no equivalent body of trained riders.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the mockery into his running account of the speech: with the greatest haughtiness imaginable Rabshakeh challenges the king to produce two thousand men who know how to manage a horse, falsely insinuating that Hezekiah has no soldiers fit for the field, and boasting that one captain of the least of his master's servants could baffle him and all his forces. The witnesses do not divide. Together they let the taunt reveal the enemy's whole strategy of contempt — to persuade the defenders that resistance is not merely dangerous but absurd, so that despair may open the gates that force has not yet breached.
24:
"How then wilt thou turn away the face of one captain of the least of my master's servants, and put thy trust on Egypt for chariots and for horsemen?" The witnesses read this as the point of the previous taunt driven home. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take it as the conclusion of the sneer about horses: if Judah cannot furnish riders for two thousand mounts, how shall she repulse even the weakest of the Assyrian officers? Charles Ellicott parses the phrase "turn away the face of" as to repulse or reject the demand of, as in 1 Kings 2:16; and "one captain of the least of my master's servants" he renders "a pasha who is one of the smallest of my lord's servants" — by which, he suggests, Rabshakeh means himself. The word rendered "captain," Ellicott notes, is the Hebrew pachath, once thought to be Persian but now known to be Semitic, from the Assyrian pahat, a prefect or provincial governor.

Ellicott would place a stop after "servants" and read the next clause as assigning the ground of Hezekiah's folly: "but thou hast put thy trust on Egypt for chariots and for horsemen," echoing the woe of Isaiah 31:1 upon those who go down to Egypt for help and stay on horses. So the two halves of the verse pin the king between contempt and reproach — too weak to face the least Assyrian officer, yet foolish enough to lean on the broken reed already exposed.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, tracing the speech as a whole, sets this verse under the same head: Rabshakeh supposes Hezekiah trusts to Egypt for chariots and horsemen, as the kings of Israel had done, and of that confidence he had already said truly, though maliciously, that it was a broken reed. The witnesses do not divide. Together they show the enemy weaving his two threads into one cord — Judah's supposed weakness and her supposed false ally — that by shaming both her arms and her alliances he may leave her, to his own mind, with nothing at all to trust; not reckoning, as the witnesses everywhere imply, on the one confidence he has all along despised, the living God of Israel.
25:
"Am I now come up without the LORD against this place to destroy it? The LORD said to me, Go up against this land, and destroy it." With this the Rabshakeh claims a divine commission, and the witnesses divide over whether anything lay behind the boast. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley is blunt: the Assyrian neither owned God's word nor regarded his providence, but forged this claim to strike terror into Hezekiah and the people. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry agrees that it was all banter and bravado — Rabshakeh did not himself believe he had any commission from God, for by whom should he have it? — yet he grants a possible colour for it, that the enemy may have heard, from the writings of the prophets, of the hand of God in the destruction of the ten tribes, and reckoned he had as good a warrant for seizing Jerusalem as for taking Samaria. Many, Henry notes, who have fought against God have pretended commissions from him.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the stratagem: seeing that the Jews placed the utmost confidence in God, Rabshakeh sought to persuade them that by Hezekiah's conduct Jehovah had departed and become the Assyrian's ally, so that they could look for no help from that quarter. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch allow that some report of the prophets' predictions, who had themselves foretold the invasion as a judgment from the Lord, may have reached the Assyrian's ears and been turned to his own use.

Charles Ellicott lays out the range of opinion: Michaelis supposed Sennacherib had consulted captive priests of the northern kingdom; others think a report of the Hebrew prophets' menaces had reached Assyrian ears; Thenius makes the words a mere inference from the run of success, though Ellicott judges the language too definite for that, and does not rule out some renegade prophet of Jehovah in the camp. He cites a striking parallel from the annals of Nabonidus, where Cyrus claims the favour and summons of Merodach against Babylon, and the oracle of Chemosh to Mesha on the Moabite Stone, "Go, seize Nebo against Israel." The very form, "Go up and destroy," Ellicott calls thoroughly authentic; and he adds that Isaiah 10:5 shows how true, in a sense far above the boaster's own apprehension, the arrogant claim really was. The witnesses do not fully divide; whether pure invention or borrowed rumour, all agree the mouth that blasphemed was, unwittingly, a rod in the hand of the God it defied.
26:
"Then said Eliakim... unto Rabshakeh, Speak, I pray thee, to thy servants in the Syrian language; for we understand it: and talk not with us in the Jews' language in the ears of the people that are on the wall." The witnesses read the ministers' request as a fearful attempt to contain the damage. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that Rabshakeh's boasting might well work upon the people, so the deputies begged him to speak in Aramaean, which they understood, and not in the Jews' tongue, for the sake of the crowd upon the wall. Aramaean, they observe, was the language of Syria and Babylonia and probably of Assyria too, perhaps the speaker's own mother-tongue, while the people of Jerusalem understood only the Hebrew of Judah; and given the closeness of the two tongues, Hebrew was not unknown to Rabshakeh, so that he could use it against them.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke cuts to the aim: the object of this blasphemous man was to stir up the people to sedition, that the city and the king might be delivered into his hand. Charles Ellicott explains why the ministers dreaded it: the people, many of whom had always worshipped at the high places, might well doubt whether there were not some truth in the charge that Jehovah was incensed at their removal. He adds the philology — Aramaic was the language of diplomacy and commerce across Western Asia, as the bilingual contract-tablets of Nineveh prove; and "the Jews' language," an expression found elsewhere only in Nehemiah 13:24, answers to the contemporary Assyrian usage that already knew the people of the south as Judeans.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry judges the request reasonable in itself, agreeable to the custom of treaties, by which envoys settle matters between themselves before anything is made public; but Hilkiah's son did not consider what an unreasonable man he had to deal with, for it did but exasperate Rabshakeh and make him the more rude and boisterous. The witnesses do not divide. Together they show the ministers rightly fearing the power of the enemy's words over an unsettled people, and rightly failing to reckon that such a man would seize their very request as his opportunity to speak all the louder to the crowd they wished to shield.
27:
"But Rabshakeh said unto them, Hath my master sent me to thy master, and to thee, to speak these words? hath he not sent me to the men which sit on the wall?" The witnesses hear the envoy fling the request back in scorn and turn deliberately to the defenders. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that Rabshakeh rejected the proposal with a sneer: his commission, he says, was not to Hezekiah and his ambassadors only but rather to the people upon the wall. In the coarse words that follow — that they may eat their own dung and drink their own water — he points to the horrors a siege of Jerusalem would bring; and they observe that the Masoretes, for reverence, substituted gentler euphemisms for the two gross terms of the text.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the threat plainly: the men are to be duly apprised that, if they hold to Hezekiah's side, Jerusalem shall be so straitly besieged that they will be reduced to eat their own excrement. John Wesley puts it in a sentence — Rabshakeh addresses the men on the wall to tell them to what extremities and miseries he will force them. Charles Ellicott renders the opening as a question, "Is it to thy lord and to thee that my lord hath sent me?", takes "the men which sit on the wall" to be the soldiers on guard, and reads the deliberately filthy language as painting the dire straits of famine in which they would be fain to appease hunger and thirst with the vilest garbage, as the horrors of the earlier siege in 2 Kings 6:25 had shown.

The witnesses do not divide. Together they mark the calculated brutality of the moment: the envoy will not keep the parley among plenipotentiaries, as courtesy and the ministers' request alike required, but turns from the negotiators to threaten the common soldiery over their heads, describing in the grossest terms the misery that awaits their fidelity. It is a stroke aimed not at the reason of the leaders but at the nerve of the men, meant to loosen their loyalty by terror; and the very indecency of it, the witnesses imply, betrays a cause that must win by fear because it cannot win by right.
28:
"Then Rabshakeh stood and cried with a loud voice in the Jews' language, and spake, saying, Hear the word of the great king, the king of Assyria." The witnesses catch the theatrical calculation of the moment. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that the verb does not mean merely that he rose or came forward, but that he stationed himself, assuming an attitude calculated for effect, and cried aloud to the people in their own tongue, bidding them hearken to the king of Assyria and not let Hezekiah lead them astray, since neither the king nor even Jehovah could deliver them from so mighty a hand. Charles Ellicott adds that he "came forward," nearer to the wall, as the phrase is used in 1 Kings 8:22, and notes that some texts read "the words" for "the word."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the design without disguise: all this was intended to cause the people to revolt from their allegiance to their king. John Wesley reports the old tradition that Rabshakeh was himself an apostate Jew — which would account for his ready command of the Jews' language — and draws the moral that, if so, his ignorance of the God of Israel was the less excusable and his enmity the less strange, for apostates are usually the most bitter and spiteful of enemies.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears again the drumbeat of "that great king," the title Rabshakeh presses a second time, exalting his master as a little god that he might overawe the people into surrender; and he too records the Jewish tradition of the apostate, comparing the peculiar spite of such men to that of the emperor Julian. The witnesses do not divide. Together they show the envoy abandoning even the pretence of negotiation and turning demagogue, raising his voice over the heads of the king's ministers to work directly on the fears of the crowd — a naked appeal to sedition, dressed in the borrowed majesty of "the great king," and aimed at the one thing the walls of Jerusalem could not by themselves defend, the loyalty and courage of the men who manned them.
29:
"Thus saith the king, Let not Hezekiah deceive you: for he shall not be able to deliver you out of his hand." The witnesses read this as the envoy's turn to the pose of a friend. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, notes how quick-witted Rabshakeh was to seize on Eliakim's unwary request and to come forward now in the character of a friend of the people, warning them against their own king; the verb "deceive" is the word used of the serpent in Genesis 3:13. He adds that "out of his hand" should be corrected, with nearly all the versions, to "out of my hand," so that the boast is the Assyrian's own.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, whose comment gathers these verses, take the drift plainly: let not Hezekiah deceive you by pretending he is able to defend or save Jerusalem, for the city cannot be delivered out of the Assyrian's hand. The whole thrust is to drive a wedge between the people and the throne, painting the king's very confidence as a fatal deception practised upon his subjects.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears the reduction of it all to a single hard alternative. To the people Rabshakeh in effect says, let not Hezekiah deceive you into your own ruin, for he shall not be able to deliver you — you must either bend or break. And Henry turns the argument, as so often, upon the sinner's dealings with God: it were well if men would submit to the force of that reasoning where it truly holds, in making their peace with God, yielding to him because it is vain to contend with him; but Rabshakeh, he insists, was not so helpless as he would represent Hezekiah, nor was the king so defenceless as the boast pretended. The witnesses do not divide. Together they lay bare the strategy of the sentence — to isolate the king, to unnerve the people, and to make trust in Hezekiah appear the surest road to destruction — a lie that the deliverance of the next chapter will expose, for the hand the envoy called irresistible was itself about to be broken.
30:
"Neither let Hezekiah make you trust in the LORD, saying, The LORD will surely deliver us, and this city shall not be delivered into the hand of the king of Assyria." The witnesses mark that the envoy now aims at the deepest root of all, the people's faith in God himself. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the two thrusts side by side: Hezekiah cannot save you himself, as v.29 had said; and now, Jehovah will not save you, as v.25 had implied. He disputes the notion that the verse betrays a merely "Jewish colouring," for if Rabshakeh could speak Hebrew he would surely know the name of the God of the Jews, and it was perfectly natural for him to assume that Hezekiah and his prophets would encourage the people to trust in the God whose sanctuary stood on Zion and who was thought bound to defend his own dwelling-place. The words, he adds, are not so exact a reproduction of the prophet's language in Isaiah 37:35 as to preclude this view; and "delivered" is better rendered "given up, yielded."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, carrying their comment across these verses, read the same design: the people are told not to let Hezekiah make them trust in Jehovah, since neither the king nor his God can hold the city against the might of Sennacherib. The assault has moved from the arm of flesh to the arm of faith itself.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sees here the enemy's chief endeavour, for he knew that the people's confidence in God was their great stay; and so, like David's enemies who laboured to drive him from his trust in God in Psalms 3:2, and like the mockers about the cross in Matthew 27:43, Rabshakeh bent all his art to shake it. The witnesses do not divide. Together they show that the last and boldest stroke of the whole speech is directed not at the walls, nor the alliances, nor the king, but at the faith by which alone the city could stand — and that in assailing it the blasphemer overreached, provoking the very God he defied.
31:
"Hearken not to Hezekiah: for thus saith the king of Assyria, Make an agreement with me by a present, and come out to me, and then eat ye every man of his own vine, and every one of his fig tree, and drink ye every one the waters of his cistern." The witnesses expose the false sweetness of the offer. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render it: make peace with me and come out to me — that is, surrender your walls — and every man shall eat of his own vine and fig-tree, till I come and take you to a land like your own. The word rendered "agreement," they note, means a blessing, peace as the sum of weal and good; and to eat one's own vine and drink one's own cistern is the standing figure of secure and undisturbed prosperity.

Charles Ellicott traces the phrase "make a blessing with me," found nowhere else, to the goodwill that commonly accompanied the making of peace, and takes "come out to me" as surrender, as in 1 Samuel 11:3 and Jeremiah 21:9. The country-folk who had fled into Jerusalem, he explains, are invited to go back to their farms and dwell in peace until Sennacherib has finished his Egyptian campaign; or, by a known idiom, "so shall ye eat, every man of his own vine," as in 1 Kings 5:5 — no harm shall befall you if you yield at once. John Wesley catches the honeyed terms: upon these conditions, says the Assyrian, I will give you no disturbance, but quietly suffer each of you to enjoy his own.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry marvels at the effrontery. With what face could Rabshakeh speak of an agreement sealed by a present, when his master had so lately broken the agreement Hezekiah made with just such a great present in v.14? Can those be trusted who have been so grossly perfidious? Yet, Henry observes, gild the chain and the vulgar will let you bind them; the devil imposes on sinners every day by just such fair temptations, promising liberty while forging fetters. The witnesses do not divide. Together they unmask the bribe — a promise of vines and fig-trees held out by the very hand that had already betrayed one bargain, and that meant, behind the smooth words, captivity all along.
32:
"Until I come and take you away to a land like your own land, a land of corn and wine, a land of bread and vineyards, a land of oil olive and of honey, that ye may live, and not die." The witnesses read the glowing catalogue as the bribe's true meaning breaking through. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch place the verse as the sequel of the promise: the surrender that begins with eating one's own vine ends with deportation to another land, however fair the terms are made to sound, and the appeal closes with the warning not to hearken to Hezekiah when he says the Lord will deliver. Charles Ellicott glosses the "oil olive" as the cultivated tree, as against the wild oleaster of 1 Kings 6:23 that yields poorer oil; takes "that ye may live" as a general promise of immunity if they obey; and renders "when he persuadeth you" as "if he prick you on," the verb of 1 Chronicles 21:1.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sees the sedition in it: the offer was well calculated to stir revolt — you cannot be delivered, your destruction is certain if you resist, but yield and Sennacherib will transport you to a land as good as your own. The bait is a fair captivity, dressed as a kindness.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry strips the promise bare. Their captivity, Rabshakeh would have them believe, would be much to their advantage, for he would take them to a land like their own land; but what the better would they be for that, Henry asks, when they must have nothing in it to call their own? So the devil, in his temptations, paints the prison as a paradise. The witnesses do not divide. Together they read the verse as the mask slipping: the promise of vines at home was only the doorway to exile abroad, and the whole soft speech resolves at last into the old Assyrian policy of transplantation, gilded over with the language of plenty so that a frightened people might walk willingly into the very ruin that had already swallowed the ten tribes.
33:
"Hath any of the gods of the nations delivered at all his land out of the hand of the king of Assyria?" With this the envoy mounts to open blasphemy, and the witnesses trace the fatal logic. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders it, "have the gods of the nations at all delivered every one his own land?" and notes a tension with the earlier claim: if the boast is to square with v.25, the thought must be that the god of each conquered nation had favoured the Assyrian, as Jehovah was there alleged to do; but since the following verses imply rather the sheer impotence of the foreign gods, a verbal inconsistency may be granted, for the rab-sak would hardly be particular in an extemporised address whose one aim was to work on the people's fears. "Out of the hand of the king of Assyria," he adds, embraces Sennacherib's predecessors as well as himself, as 2 Kings 19:12 shows.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch put the argument in its bald form: even Jehovah, Rabshakeh reasons, could no more deliver Jerusalem than Hezekiah could; and to prove it he begins to enumerate the cities and lands the Assyrian had conquered while their gods stood by helpless — "where are the gods of Hamath?" — the question meaning that they are gone, have perished, and could not save.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lays the blasphemy open. The envoy compares the God of Israel with the gods of the nations he had subdued and reckons him no more able to defend his worshippers than they had been. Here, Henry says, is his pride — for when he conquered a city he counted himself to have conquered its gods — and here his profaneness, that he ranks the only living and true God, the God of the whole earth, with the upstart fictitious gods of the heathen. The witnesses do not divide. Together they see the boaster climbing, verse by verse, toward the height of his impiety, and reckoning by a false arithmetic that would soon be answered, for the God he numbered among the idols was about to prove himself alone.
34:
"Where are the gods of Hamath, and of Arpad? where are the gods of Sepharvaim, Hena, and Ivah? have they delivered Samaria out of mine hand?" The witnesses meet the roll-call of fallen cities with a mixture of history and horror. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke states the blasphemy at its plainest: Sennacherib boasts himself greater than any of the gods of the nations, for the Assyrians had already overthrown the gods of Hamath, Arpad, Hena, and Ivah, so that Jehovah, he argues, shall be like one of them and unable to deliver Jerusalem. The impudence of this speech, Clarke says, is without parallel, and Hezekiah rightly treated it as an insult aimed not at himself but at the Lord.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott supplies the record behind the names: Sargon, Sennacherib's father, had reduced Hamath and Arpad, and his annals tell how Yahubihdi, king of the Hamathites, drew Arpad, Damascus, and Samaria into revolt, was defeated at Qarqar, and flayed alive. Arpad he locates north of Aleppo; Sepharvaim he refers to 2 Kings 17:24. Over Hena and Ivah the witnesses divide. Ellicott notes that these names, absent from the parallel in Isaiah, look at first like two Hebrew verbs — "he made to wander, and overturned" — which the Targum indeed renders as a question, and which Hoffmann would read as a single epithet of Sepharvaim; yet Ellicott himself, with the versions, prefers to take them as genuine place-names, the Avva and the Avites of 2 Kings 17:24. The "where are the gods?" he reads as implying their annihilation with their cities, though the Assyrians sometimes carried idols off rather than destroyed them, as Isaiah 10:11 allows.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch join the verse to its conclusion in the next, reading the whole as the argument that no god of the conquered lands had rescued his land, so that Jehovah too, the boaster infers, will not defend Jerusalem. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry adds that the God of Israel is no local deity to be ranked with the upstart gods of Hamath and Arpad, but the God of the whole earth. The witnesses do not divide on the blasphemy; they differ only over the obscure names of Hena and Ivah, whether places wiped out or verbs of ruin.
35:
"Who are they among all the gods of the countries, that have delivered their country out of mine hand, that the LORD should deliver Jerusalem out of mine hand?" This is the summit of the Rabshakeh's blasphemy, and the witnesses gather to answer it. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott glosses "the countries" as those the Assyrian had himself conquered, and notes that Ewald reads the closing clause as "much less will Jehovah deliver," taking the particle as equivalent to "how much more." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch draw out the inference the verse itself makes: as not one of the gods of the lands has been able to rescue his land from Assyria, so, the boaster concludes, Jehovah also will be unable to defend Jerusalem — the God of Israel levelled at a stroke with the idols of the nations.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke names the sin for what it is: the impudent blasphemy of this speech is without parallel, for it sets the living God on a level with the vanities of the heathen; and Hezekiah, treating it as directed not against himself but against the Lord, refers the whole matter to Jehovah, who would punish the blasphemy in the most signal manner. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry marks the profaneness of it. The God of Israel was no local deity, but the God of the whole earth, the only living and true God, the Ancient of Days, who had often proved himself above all gods; yet Rabshakeh makes no more of him than of the upstart gods of Hamath and Arpad, arguing that because they could not defend their worshippers neither could he. One grain of sincerity, Henry says, would have been worth all this wit and rhetoric.

The witnesses do not divide. Together they let the verse stand as the boast that overreached itself: by dragging the name of Jehovah into the list of conquered idols, the Assyrian passed from insulting a king to defying the Almighty, and so turned a war of empire into a contest with heaven. The very completeness of the blasphemy, the witnesses imply, seals its answer; for the God who will not give his glory to another had now been openly challenged to prove that he is not as the gods of Hamath, and the next chapter will record how terribly he proved it.
36:
"But the people held their peace, and answered him not a word: for the king's commandment was, saying, Answer him not." The witnesses read the silence as discipline, not defeat. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain that the people were wholly silent at the address, because Hezekiah had forbidden them to answer — partly to keep Rabshakeh from saying yet more, and partly that the Assyrian's envoys might be left in complete uncertainty as to what impression their words had made; and the deputies returned to the king with their clothes rent, in grief that not only Hezekiah but still more Jehovah had been blasphemed.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the command as a wise committing of the cause to God: answer him not, for the blasphemy is too barefaced; it is Jehovah who is insulted, not you, so let him avenge his own quarrel — a matter, Clarke adds, that the next chapter will take up.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry dwells on the reasons and the wisdom of the silence. They held their peace, he says, not for want of something to say, for they might justly have upbraided the Assyrian with his master's treachery and breach of faith; but the king had commanded it, and there is a time to keep silence as well as a time to speak. There are those, Henry observes, to whom to offer anything religious or rational is but to cast pearls before swine; what can be said to a madman? He grants that their silence likely made Rabshakeh the more proud and secure, and so hardened him to his own destruction — yet a decided expression of abhorrence, and the leaving of such a blasphemer to rail, is often the best testimony against him, the matter being left with the Lord who has all hearts in his hand. The witnesses do not divide. Together they show a besieged people mastering the hardest discipline of faith — to make no answer at all to insult and provocation — and referring their whole cause, in a believing and deliberate silence, to the God whose honour was more deeply wounded than their own, and who alone could fitly reply.
37:
"Then came Eliakim the son of Hilkiah, which was over the household, and Shebna the scribe, and Joah the son of Asaph the recorder, to Hezekiah with their clothes rent, and told him the words of Rabshakeh." The witnesses close the chapter on the rent garments and the faithful report. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply record the three ministers coming back to the king with their clothes torn, the outward sign of their grief at the blasphemy they had been forced to hear, and telling him all that the Assyrian had said.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke explains the custom and its meaning. It was the way of the Hebrews, on hearing any blasphemy, to rend their clothes, for this was the greatest of crimes, immediately affecting the majesty of God; and a religious people rightly held in the utmost abhorrence every insult offered to the object of their worship. Then Clarke traces the chain of mediation that the torn robes set in motion: these three ambassadors lay the matter before the king as God's representative; the king lays it before the prophet as God's minister; and the prophet lays it before God as the people's mediator — so that the blasphemy uttered at the wall is carried, step by step, up to the throne of heaven.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the same threads: they rent their clothes in detestation of the blasphemy and in grief for the despised and afflicted condition of Jerusalem, whose reproach lay heavy upon them, and they faithfully reported the whole matter to their master, that he might consider what was to be done. The witnesses do not divide. Together they let the chapter end on a note of sorrow that is not yet despair: the insult has been heard and borne in silence, the loyal servants have brought it home with torn garments, and the burden is now laid where alone it can be answered — before the king, and through him before the God whose name was blasphemed, whose reply the next chapter will unfold. The rent clothes are both mourning and prayer, the first movement of a faith that turns its wound into intercession.
